title: 'Annihilation,' 'Ex Machina,' and Alex Garland's Disturbing Sci-Fi Vision
format: Sean Fennessey sits down with writer-director Alex Garland to talk through Annihilation, Ex Machina, and the screenwriting years before them.

films.title: Annihilation
films.note: The new one, a $40M adaptation of Jeff VanderMeer's novel. Garland calls it a metaphysical, hallucinogenic atmosphere piece he built around self-destruction, and told VanderMeer up front he couldn't do a faithful version.

films.title: Ex Machina
films.note: His directing debut, described here as a Swiss watch of a film: four people, one house, $15M. A surprise hit that Garland says looked like it would lose money before it didn't.

films.title: Dredd
films.note: The drug-fueled, ultra-violent one before Ex Machina. The only time Garland tried a franchise, and he was relieved when the sequel never happened.

films.title: Never Let Me Go
films.note: The small, sad, literary adaptation he wrote before Dredd, part of a run he says lost money three films in a row.

films.title: Sunshine
films.note: One of the back-to-back films Garland lists as box-office losses, alongside Never Let Me Go and Dredd.

films.title: 28 Days Later
films.note: The first film he wrote, a reaction against The Beach. His schooling in filmmaking came from working with director Danny Boyle here.

films.title: The Beach
films.note: The adaptation of his first novel, which he felt was too soft. 28 Days Later was his push toward more aggression and punk attitude.
